content.full_text:
October 6, 1980 
Mr. Alan Edelstein, Legal Counsel 
California Teamsters Public Affairs Council 
925 L St. t Suite 920 
Sacramento, California 95814 
Dear Mr. Edelstein: 
This letter will confirm advice I provided to you during our 
telephone conversation of September 19, 1980. 
In your letter of 
September 23, 1980. you explained that the California Teamsters 
Public Affairs Council is considering using some of its funds, 
which are currently in non-interest bearing accounts, to purchase 
certificates of deposits or !reasury Bills. 
Your specific ques-
tions were: 
1. What restrictions, if any, are there on the investments 
of your funds? 
2. 
How should the money earned on the funds be indicated 
when filed under requirements of the Political Reform Act. 
3. 
How should the principle invested be indicated when 
you file? 
Under the Political Reform Act, there are no restrictions on the 
investment of funds by political committees. 
The money earned on 
the invested funds would be reported as of the day of receipt by 
your committee on Schedule G (miscellaneous adjustments to cash 
position) on Form 420. 
A note should be included on Schedule E 
(payments and contributions made) at the time the certificate of 
deposit or Treasury Bills are purchased stating the type and amount 
of the investment, but the amount of the investment will not appear 
in the amount paid column. 
Your cash on hand, therefore, will het 
be adjusted by the amount of the investment I but only by the money 
earned on the invested funds. 
If you have any additional questions, please contact me. 
Yours truly, 
Lynn Montgomery 
Staff Services Analyst 


I 
I 
-' ::.) .' I , 
Ms. Lynn Montgomery 
Technical Assistance 
and Analysis Division 
September 23, 1980 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street - P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Dear Ms. Montgomery: 
Pursuant to our conversation of September 19, 1980, 
I am requesting a written explanation of the require-
ments and procedures pertaining to money earned by 
our organization. 
The California Teamsters Public Affairs Council employs 
lobbyists and it makes contributions to California 
legislative campaigns. 
The Council IS funds are now 
deposited in non-interest bearing accounts. 
The Council 
is now considering depositing some of its funds in 
interest bearing savings accounts, and it is considering 
purchasing certificates of deposit or Treasury Bills. 
Specifically, we have the following three questions: 
1. 
What restrictions, if any, are there on the 
investment of our funds? 
2. 
How should the money earned on the funds be 
indicated when we file under FPPC requirements. 
3. 
How should the principle invested be indicated 
when we file? 
Thank you for your attention to this matter. 
ALE:deh 
Sincerely, 
Alan L. Edelstein 
Legal Counsel

sections.question: 1. What restrictions, if any, are there on the investments of your funds? 2. How should the money earned on the funds be indicated when filed under requirements of the Political Reform Act. 3. How should the principle invested be indicated when you file?
sections.conclusion: Under the Political Reform Act, there are no restrictions on the investment of funds by political committees. The money earned on the invested funds would be reported as of the day of receipt by your committee on Schedule G (miscellaneous adjustments to cash position) on Form 420. A note should be included on Schedule E (payments and contributions made) at the time the certificate of deposit or Treasury Bills are purchased stating the type and amount of the investment, but the amount of the investment will not appear in the amount paid column. Your cash on hand, therefore, will not be adjusted by the amount of the investment but only by the money earned on the invested funds.
sections.question_synthetic: The California Teamsters Public Affairs Council seeks clarification on restrictions for investing committee funds in certificates of deposit or Treasury Bills, and how to properly report both the earned income and principal invested amounts under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determined that political committees face no restrictions on investing funds and should report earnings on Schedule G while documenting the investment details on Schedule E without affecting the cash on hand column. Only the earned income, not the principal investment amount, adjusts the reported cash position.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (September 23, 1980 letter) and FPPC response (October 6, 1980 letter). Extracted content from the FPPC response as the primary advice letter. All key sections (questions and conclusion) were clearly identified and extracted verbatim.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. What restrictions, if any, are there on the investments of your funds? 2. How should the money earned on the funds be indicated when filed under requirements of the Political Reform Act. 3. How should the principle invested be indicated when you file?

CONCLUSION: Under the Political Reform Act, there are no restrictions on the investment of funds by political committees. The money earned on the invested funds would be reported as of the day of receipt by your committee on Schedule G (miscellaneous adjustments to cash position) on Form 420. A note should be included on Schedule E (payments and contributions made) at the time the certificate of deposit or Treasury Bills are purchased stating the type and amount of the investment, but the amount of the investment will not appear in the amount paid column. Your cash on hand, therefore, will not be adjusted by the amount of the investment but only by the money earned on the invested funds.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to the California Teamsters Public Affairs Council's inquiry regarding investment of committee funds in interest-bearing accounts, clarifying that no restrictions exist and providing specific reporting requirements for both investment income and principal.
